summary: This notebook demonstrates an inference pipeline for the EEDI competition using a fine-tuned `bge-large-en-v1.5` model to retrieve relevant misconceptions for math questions. It constructs semantic query strings by concatenating question metadata and answer options, encodes them alongside a misconception vocabulary, and ranks the top 25 matches per query using cosine similarity. The author notes that while the approach achieves a competitive ~10th place score, the lack of validation data makes early stopping difficult to determine. It serves as a practical baseline showing how dense retrieval embeddings can be applied to structured educational data without complex tabular modeling.
competition_flow: Loads test questions and answer options, formats them into semantic text strings, encodes them alongside a misconception vocabulary using a fine-tuned BGE model, computes cosine similarity to rank the top 25 misconceptions per query, filters out correct answers, and exports the ranked IDs as a CSV submission.
data_reading: Reads `test.csv` and `misconception_mapping.csv` from the local Kaggle input directory using `polars.read_csv`.
data_processing: Unpivots answer columns (A-D) into long format, concatenates metadata and answer text into a single string with semantic tags, normalizes embeddings during encoding, computes cosine similarity to rank misconceptions, and filters out rows matching the correct answer.
insights: Fine-tuning a dense retrieval model on educational text pairs effectively captures semantic relationships for misconception prediction.
Formatting raw text with explicit semantic tags improves embedding quality and retrieval performance.
The absence of validation data makes determining optimal training epochs challenging, highlighting the need for proxy metrics in similar retrieval tasks.
